content.problemStatement:
Consider performing the following operation on SSS - a binary string of length NNN:


Choose XXX such that 1<X<N1 < X < N1<X<N and SX=1S_X = 1SX​=1.
Reverse the subarray S[X−1,X+1]S[X - 1, X + 1]S[X−1,X+1], i.e. effectively swapping SX−1S_{X - 1}SX−1​ and SX+1S_{X + 1}SX+1​.
Let TTT denote another binary string of length NNN, and f(S,T)f(S, T)f(S,T) be the minimum number of operations to convert SSS into TTT with the above operation. If SSS cannot be covered to TTT, f(S,T)=0f(S, T) = 0f(S,T)=0.

Given NNN and a string SSS of length NNN, find the sum of f(S,T)f(S, T)f(S,T) over all 2N2^N2N binary strings TTT of length NNN.  Since the answer may be large, print it modulo 998244353998244353998244353.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.

The first line contains NNN - the length of the string.
The second line contains SSS - the binary string.




Output Format
For each test case, output a single integer, the sum of f(S,T)f(S, T)f(S,T) over all 2N2^N2N binary strings TTT modulo 998244353998244353998244353.
constraints: The sum of NNN over all test cases does not exceed 500050005000.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test Case 1 : There are 888 binary strings, out of which 666 cannot be obtained so by default their contribution is 000.
The 222 binary strings that can be obtained are T=110T = 110T=110 and T=011T = 011T=011. The first is the original string itself, so f(S,T)=0f(S, T) = 0f(S,T)=0. The second can be obtained by one operation at X=2X = 2X=2.
Thus, the sum is 0⋅6+0+1=10 \cdot 6 + 0 + 1 = 10⋅6+0+1=1.
Test Case 2 : The only obtainable binary string is the original string itself, i.e. 100100100 and this has f(S,T)=0f(S, T) = 0f(S,T)=0.
